coredata.dc:title: Enzymeless glucose sensor based on disposable Ecoflex®/graphite thermoplastic composite substrate modified with Au@GQDs

de Araujo, William R.
coredata.dc:description: There is an increasing demand for robust, affordable, and sensitive analytical devices for clinical diagnostics. Herein, we describe a simple route to produce biodegradable and disposable thermoplastic composite electrodes using the commercial polymer (Ecoflex®) and graphite microparticles. The surface of the carbon composite electrode was modified with gold nanoparticles conjugated with graphene quantum dots (Au@GQDs) for sensitive and non-enzymatic glucose sensing. The morphological and structural characterizations of the modified electrochemical device revealed a hydrophilic rough composite surface, with high dispersion of graphite in the polymer matrix and confirmed the successful superficial modification of the working electrode with spherical Au@GQDs nanoparticles. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) were used to electrochemically characterize the modified and nonmodified thermoplastic sensor, demonstrating a 4.5-fold enhancement of the electroactive area, higher heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics, and electrocatalytic properties for glucose sensing when the sensor was modified with Au@GQDs. The electrochemical determination of the glucose was carried out using an amperometric method applying -0.15 V in alkaline conditions (0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH). Under optimized conditions, the proposed method presented a high sensitivity (4.85 A L mol−1) and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 9.12 µmol L− 1. The Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensor was applied for glucose sensing in synthetic saliva samples with recovery values ranging from 97.84% to 105.20%, highlighting the excellent accuracy of the proposed method. Finally, the electrode was subjected to thermal and biological degradation studies and presented disposable and eco-friendly characteristics. Based on these results, we believe that this new thermoplastic composite sensor modified with Au@GQDs can be an excellent candidate for a disposable, low-cost, and enzymeless electrochemical glucose sensor.

(A) Schematic representation of the main manufacturing steps of the composite sensors in the optimized conditions, (B) schematic representation of the modification of the working electrode using Au@GQDs dispersion, and a real image of the final device.

Characterization of the composite sensor before and after modification with GQDs and Au@GQDs. (A-C) Wettability study. SEM images of the (D) Ecoflex, and (E) Ecoflex:graphite electrode. STEM images of (F) GQDs dispersion at 160,000x magnification, and (G) Au@GQDs dispersion at 1200,000x magnification. Inserted the size distribution histogram of Au@GQDs.

(A) Absorption spectra obtained for GQDs (black line) and Au@GQDs (red line). (B-C) emission spectra of GQDs before and after conjugating with AuNPs. (D) Digital images showing the color of Au@GQDs and GQDs dispersions and the difference of fluorescence intensity observed under UV light exposure, (A) Au@GQDs and (B) GQDs.

Electrochemical study of the composite electrochemical sensor (A) Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 mmol L− 1 paraquat in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl and (B) Nyquist plots of a mixture of 5.0 mmol L− 1 [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl for (●) Ecoflex-graphite, (●) Ecoflex-graphite/GQDs, and (●) Ecoflex-graphite/Au@ GQDs. Inset in B is a plot of the magnified region of the semi-arcs. Parameters: scan rate at (A) was 50 mV s− 1; frequency range in (B) was 1 × 105 to 0.1 Hz and an amplitude of 10 mV.

(A) Chronoamperogram recorded under stirring condition using the composite electrode modified with Au@GQDs for successive additions of glucose and (B) analytical curve obtained by standard addition of glucose levels ranging from 0.1 mmol L− 1 to 1.5 mmol L− 1 in 0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH applying −0.15 V.

Electrode degradation studies. (A) TGA analysis of the main constituents of the composite electrode (Ecoflex®, graphite and silver). (B) FTIR spectrum of Ecoflex® before exposure to UV-C light (0 h) and after 8 days of photodegradation (192 h). (C) Photographic records of the process of biodegradation of electrodes when buried in the soil. C-1, photodegraded electrodes for 120 h, inside a plastic Petri dish with holes that were used to bury the samples. C-2, unearthed electrodes. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an increasing demand for robust, affordable, and sensitive analytical devices for clinical diagnostics. Herein, we describe a simple route to produce biodegradable and disposable thermoplastic composite electrodes using the commercial polymer (Ecoflex®) and graphite microparticles. The surface of the carbon composite electrode was modified with gold nanoparticles conjugated with graphene quantum dots (Au@GQDs) for sensitive and non-enzymatic glucose sensing. The morphological and structural characterizations of the modified electrochemical device revealed a hydrophilic rough composite surface, with high dispersion of graphite in the polymer matrix and confirmed the successful superficial modification of the working electrode with spherical Au@GQDs nanoparticles. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) were used to electrochemically characterize the modified and nonmodified thermoplastic sensor, demonstrating a 4.5-fold enhancement of the electroactive area, higher heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics, and electrocatalytic properties for glucose sensing when the sensor was modified with Au@GQDs. The electrochemical determination of the glucose was carried out using an amperometric method applying -0.15 V in alkaline conditions (0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH). Under optimized conditions, the proposed method presented a high sensitivity (4.85 A L mol−1) and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 9.12 µmol L− 1. The Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensor was applied for glucose sensing in synthetic saliva samples with recovery values ranging from 97.84% to 105.20%, highlighting the excellent accuracy of the proposed method. Finally, the electrode was subjected to thermal and biological degradation studies and presented disposable and eco-friendly characteristics. Based on these results, we believe that this new thermoplastic composite sensor modified with Au@GQDs can be an excellent candidate for a disposable, low-cost, and enzymeless electrochemical glucose sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: The monitoring of glucose in biological fluids is widely employed for diabetes monitoring and management since high levels of this metabolite can generate additional problems in the heart, blindness, kidney failure, and other complications. On the other hand, low levels of glucose in the body can also lead to health problems and brain damage [1]. The normal glucose levels for a healthy individual should be between 0.056 and 0.83 mM in urine [2], 0.050 and 0.15 mM in saliva [3], and around 3.9 and 7.1 mM in blood [4]. According to World Health Organization (WHO) is estimated around 366 million people will have diabetes in 2030 worldwide [5], highlighting the importance of fast and low-cost glucose tests.

Several analytical methods are widely used to quantify and monitor glucose, such as spectroscopy [6], surface plasmon resonance [7], colorimetry [8], capacitive detection [9], and fluorescence [10,11]. However, some methods require a long working time, skilled operators, high cost of analysis, and complex operational procedures. Therefore, electrochemical sensors emerged as an important analytical tool to provide rapid response, high sensitivity, and low-cost [12–15]. Currently, the most used electrochemical sensors are based on the modification of the working electrode surface using biological receptors, such as proteins [16], antibodies [17], and enzymes [18–20]. However, the bioactivity of the biological receptor can be affected by environmental conditions, such as pH, humidity, temperature, among others [21,22].

Non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors are attractive for in-field analysis and alternative methods to measure glucose in biological matrices, like wearable devices [23]. Parashuram and collaborators [24] reported a non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor based on ZrO2:Cu(I) nanosphere modified carbon paste electrode for detection of glucose in raw Citrus aurantium using chronoamperometry technique. The authors obtained a limit of detection (LOD) of 250 μmol L− 1 in a concentration range from 1 mmol L− 1 to 10 mmol L− 1. Mohapatra and collaborators [25] developed a non-enzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on carbon nano-onions to detect glucose in PBS buffer using chronoamperometry. The electrochemical sensor showed a LOD of 90 μmol L− 1, with a wide linear concentration range from 2 mmol L− 1 to 28 mmol L− 1. However, several works reported in the literature made use of expensive materials or complex material synthesis to manufacture the glucose sensors, which becomes the analysis more expensive and unviable for routine clinical diagnostic.

The advantage of using electrochemical sensors based on the biodegradable thermoplastic composites material occurs due to characteristics such as low cost, flexibility, eco-friendly, easy methods for electrode manufacture, and disposability [26,27]. Ecoflex® ((poly (butylene-adipate-co-terephthalate)), is a synthetic copolyester, obtained by polycondensation between butanediol (BDO), adipic acid (AA), and terephthalic acid (PTA). It is part of the aliphatic-aromatic polyester class, which is highly biodegradable due to ester bonds in the chain, which are sensitive to hydrolysis, has a hydrophobic nature, high flexibility, good processability, in addition to biocompatibility [28]. The mechanical properties are derived from aromatic polyesters and are like those of polyethylene, which is a polyolefin widely used worldwide [29]. The study of Ecoflex material has gained prominence in recent years and has been used to compose blends with biopolymers [30], aiming at the synergy of properties and obtaining eco-friendly materials [26], with competitive costs for several applications.

In this work, we describe a simple route to fabricate a high-performance thermoplastic composite sensor using the biodegradable polymer commercially known as Ecoflex® and graphite microparticles. The composite sensor was manufactured based on the solvent-assisted method [31,32]. The working electrode (WE) was modified by gold nanoparticles conjugated with graphene quantum dots (Au@GQDs) using the drop-casting technique for sensitive and selective glucose sensing in synthetic saliva samples.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade. Poly (butylene-adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT) was supplied by BASF under the trade name of Ecoflex®. Synthetic Graphite powder (<20 μm, assay 99%, Alfa Aesar), tetrachloroauric(III) acid (HAuCl4.3H2O), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium chloride (KCl), 1,1′-Dimethyl-4,4′-bipyridinium dichloride (paraquat), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), citric acid and glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chloroform (GC grade) was purchased from Synth. Ag/AgCl conductive ink was acquired from Creative Materials. All stock solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (Sartorius Weighing Technology, Germany) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using a galvanostatic/potentiostat, model PGSTAT30 from Autolab (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was used to characterize the electrochemical behavior of the modified and nonmodified sensor in the presence of 5.0 mmol L− 1 paraquat in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl medium. The glucose electrooxidation was recorded in alkaline conditions (0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH). EIS was used to electrochemically characterize the modified and nonmodified sensor and the obtained resistance to charge transfer (Rct) was used to compare the electrodes. The EIS experiments were performed in 5.0 mmol L− 1 [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl. The glucose quantification was performed by amperometric measurements applying −0.15 V, which was conducted in 0.10 mol L− 1 NaOH at 25 °C with different concentrations of glucose ranging from 0.10 mmol L− 1 to 1.50 mmol L− 1.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to the protocol of Dong and coauthors [33]. Briefly, 1.0 g of citric acid was weighed and transferred to a hydrothermal flask and kept at 200 °C for 30 min. Afterward, the orange solution color was observed and then, 50.0 mL of a 0.25 mol L− 1 NaOH solution was added drop-to-drop and kept under stirring. Thus, the orange color changed to straw yellow indicating the GQDs formation [34].

For the synthesis of graphene quantum dots conjugated with gold nanoparticles (Au@GQDs), 6.25 mL of GQDs solution were transferred into a volumetric flask of 25.0 mL and completed with deionized water. This solution was heated to 94 °C with continuous stirring and then, 250.0 µL of 10.0 mg mL−1 HAuCl4 solution was added slowly (drop-to-drop) until it yield a wine-colored Au@GQDs suspension, which was cooled quickly. Both suspensions (GQDs and Au@GQDs) were stored in a refrigerator at 8 °C in an amber flask for a maximum period of 30 days [35]. Fig. 1 illustrates a schematic representation of GQDs and Au@GQDs syntheses based on the hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the flexible composite electrodes and modification with Au@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ecoflex:graphite composite substrate was manufactured based on the solvent-assisted method [31,32]. Briefly, 1.00 g of Ecoflex® was added to a volumetric flask with 10.0 mL of chloroform, under constant stirring until complete dissolution. Afterward, 2.33 g of graphite powder was added to the flask and kept stirring for 10 min in a sonicator. The mixture was transferred to a petri dish with a diameter of 7 cm, which was kept static at room temperature (25 °C) until complete solvent evaporation and substrate formation. The thermoplastic substrate was positioned in the manual press for mechanical compression applying 10 tons for 10 min.

The composite substrate has been cut in electrodes format and attached to the Ecoflex® sheet, which was prepared by adding 2.0 g of Ecoflex® to a volumetric flask with 10.0 mL of chloroform until complete dissolution. The mixture was transferred to a petri dish and dried at room temperature. Conductive Ag/AgCl ink was employed to manufacture a pseudo-reference electrode and it was cured at 100 °C for 30 min in a vacuum oven. The working electrode surface was modified with 30.0 µL GQDs or Au@GQDs by drop-casting technique and dried at room temperature overnight before use in electrochemical measurements. Fig. 2 illustrates the main manufacturing steps of the composite substrate and its modification with Au@GQDs nanomaterials.

GQDs and Au@GQDs were investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy in a Thermo scientific, Genesys 10UV Scanning, and by fluorescence spectroscopy in a Shimadzu RF5301pc equipment, employing λex = 338 nm and emission scanning from 330 to 490 nm. The wettability of the composite substrate was measured by the sessile drop technique in a Ramé Hart goniometer (model 100–00) with deionized water. For each sample, three droplets of water have been deposited on different points of the substrate surface, and 10 contact angle measurements were recorded for each droplet, i.e., the mean results correspond to the average of 30 measurements. The structural characterization was carried out employing Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) Nicolet summit IR 200 FT-IR in reflectance mode, using 120 scans, nominal resolution of 4.0 cm−1, in the range of 500 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy was performed on a Horiba T64000 confocal with 532 nm laser, 30 mW power, 100x objective, 200 μm aperture, 100 μm slit, 15 s of exposure, and 5 scans accumulation in the range of 1000 to 3200 cm−1. The morphology characterizations of different substrates and nanocomposites were carried out by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM), model TM3000, with an applied acceleration voltage of 20 kV. Peaks ≥ 0.4 cps/eV (electron-volt counts per second) were considered over the entire 0 to 10 keV (kilo-electron-volt) spectrum. The thermal characterization was performed by thermogravimetry analysis (TGA). The equipment used was a Perkin Elmer, model Pyris1 TGA, at a constant heating rate of 10 °C/min, from 25 to 900 °C, in a synthetic air atmosphere.

Synthetic saliva was prepared by mixing 17.2 mg CaCl2, 13.2 mg NaCl, 96.4 mg KCl, 20.4 mg urea, 17.4 mg lactic acid, and 65.0 mg KH2PO4 in a volumetric flask in 100 mL deionized water. This solution was spiked with several concentrations of glucose for electrochemical sensing, similarly to previous studies [36,37]. For quantitative amperometric analyses, the prepared saliva samples were diluted in 0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH electrolyte solution directly in the electrochemical cell. Furthermore, the interference studies were performed with the individual addition of 0.30 mmol L− 1 tartrazine, sodium benzoate, nitrite, sodium bisulfite, fructose, and ascorbic acid, which are potential concomitant molecules found in saliva or by ingestion after meals.

To analyze the degradation behavior of the sensors after disposal, studies of photodegradation, thermal degradation, and biodegradation of the samples were carried out. For photodegradation analysis, post-use sensors were exposed to UV-C light for a period of 8 days in a labmade photodegradation chamber (details in the Supporting Information - S1). Changes in surface composition were monitored by FTIR analysis, reading 4 points, with different compositions of the same electrode, before degradation and after 192 h of UV-C radiation exposure. The thermal degradation study was carried out by thermogravimetry analysis, where the mass loss of the material as a function of temperature variation was measured. The study by the biodegradation of the electrode material was carried out in the soil, for which electrodes were buried for 6 months. The record of the evolution of the degradation of the materials was carried out through photographs.

The analysis of the materials composition was performed by FTIR analysis, Figure S2A, seeking to observe the changes that occurred in the Ecoflex:graphite composite electrode with the functionalization of the substrate surface. The spectrum of the Ecoflex:graphite sample shows a characteristic band at 2950 cm−1 related to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH2 groups. In addition to an intense band at 1710 cm−1 which refers to the stretching vibration of C–O. The band at 1395 cm−1 is related to the stretching trans-CH2-plane bending vibration, and in 1100 cm−1, C–O left-right symmetric stretching vibration absorption. It is possible to observe these bands even with the introduction of graphite in the polymer matrix [38]. The spectra of the Ecoflex:graphite electrode functionalized with the GQDs exhibited absorption bands of the carboxyl group and the hydroxyl group at 1710 cm−1 and 3300 −3600 cm−1, respectively, showing that both contain –C = O groups. It is possible to observe a new band in the region of 3300 cm−1 characteristics to absorption of the aromatic compound and of C–H stretching vibration in aromatic rings, and of skeletal vibration of aromatic rings in the range of 1450 to 1650 cm−1 [39]. Based on the changes observed when comparing the FTIR spectrum of the Ecoflex:graphite composite before and after the surface modification with GQDs or Au@GQDs, it is possible to point out that there was a modification of the substrate in both cases because of the appearance and changes in the intensity of the bands, which indicate the modification of the material with new types of bonds and the presence of the GQDs and Au@GQDs in the modified thermoplastic composite sensor [40,41].

Structural analyses of Ecoflex:graphite and the modifiers were performed by Raman spectroscopy, which is a technique widely used to characterize carbonaceous materials [42,43]. Figure S2B shows Raman spectra obtained for Ecoflex:graphite, GQDs, and Au@GQDs. The thermoplastic composite electrode presented a high-intensity sp2 carbon domain (G band at 1577 cm–1) related to graphite microparticles content and a low-intensity D band at 1322 cm−1 associated with the sp3 domain of the polymer and structural defects. Thus, the composite material presented low structural defects and stacked layers since the intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) was 0.14 and exhibited a broadened G′ band, respectively. The Raman spectra of the GQDs show an ID/IG ratio of 0.95, which confirms the presence of disorder on the edges or functional groups on the GQDs surface [44]. To Au@GQDs Raman spectra, we can see two more intensive bands located at 1176 and 1695 cm−1, which is attributed to SERS spectra of gold nanoparticles [45]. Through Eq. (1) and the ID/IG ratio, it is possible to calculate the crystalline domain of the GQDs and Ecoflex:graphite [31,46].

where La is the crystalline domain size, and λ is the wavelength of the laser.

The La values obtained for Ecoflex:graphite and GQDs were 137 and 20 nm, respectively, which is the average inter-defect spacings (La) reported in the literature for GQDs (ranging from 1 nm to 24 nm) [47] and thermoplastic composite sensor containing graphite [31,42].

The changes in wettability of the produced sensor (Ecoflex:graphite) after surface functionalization with GQDs or Au@GQDs were also analyzed. Fig. 3 A-C shows the image of the droplet and the measured contact angle values. The Ecoflex:graphite electrode presented a contact angle of 67.25° ± 0.33° (Fig. 3A), the Ecoflex:graphite/GQDs functionalized electrodes exhibited 58.80° ± 0.75° (Fig. 3B), and Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs material presented a contact angle of 47.95° ± 0.30° (Fig. 3C). The smaller contact angle values ​indicate an increase in wettability of the composite electrode surface with the nanomaterials functionalization. This modification indicates that there was a reduction in the cohesion force, which seeks to contain the scattering of droplets on a surface. This result is highly desired since the greater wettability can contribute to a better interaction of the electrode with the aqueous medium commonly used in electrochemical measurements.

The micrographs of the Ecoflex polymer and the composite material are shown in Fig. 3D-E. The Ecoflex shows a flat morphology like that reported in the literature for this biopolymer [48]. The observation of Ecoflex:graphite composite morphology (Fig. 3E) revealed a rougher surface, with excellent dispersion/percolation of graphite microparticles in the Ecoflex polymer matrix, since the latter acts as a binder for the graphite microparticles filler [49], which presented ordered morphology of graphite microparticles, that is responsible for producing a large active surface area in the sensor [31,50]. The morphology of the GQDs and Au@GQDs nanoparticle's dispersion was also evaluated by STEM (Figs. 3F and 3G). It is possible to note spherical nanostructures with homogeneous distribution and dimensions for both nanomaterials. Fig. 3G illustrates the Au@GQDs at 1200,000x magnification, from which was possible to define the spherical shape and obtain the size distribution histogram, where the average size of Au@GQDs was calculated as 7.39 ± 0.65 nm, with an R2 of 0.999, and a polydispersion of 8.80%.

The synthesized nanomaterials were also characterized by spectroscopy techniques. Fig. 4 A shows the absorbance spectra of GQDs (black line) and Au@GQDs (red line). GQDs demonstrate a sharp band at 338 nm, which is due to the contribution to the π–π* transition of the C–C bond of the HOMO-LUMO orbital [41]. When the AuNPs were incorporated onto GQDs, we obtained an absorption band at 532 nm characteristic of AuNPs [19,51,52] and decreased the GQDs band at 338 nm [53] due to adsorption of the metallic nanoparticles onto their surfaces. Fig. 4B and 4C illustrate the fluorescence spectra of GQDs and Au@GQDs, respectively. When the GQDs dispersion is irradiated by ultraviolet (UV) light, the colorless GQDs dispersion exhibited a strong emission band at 390 nm (Fig. 4B). A large decrease of fluorescence intensity was obtained in Au@GQDs suspension, which is an indication of the successful combination of GQDs and AuNPs, resulting in a significant fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4C). The difference in fluorescence intensity can be easily identified by the naked eye (Fig. 4D). These optical characteristics corroborate with the results obtained by other authors that used similar nanomaterials for other applications [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of the thermoplastic composite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The unmodified thermoplastic composite sensor (Ecoflex:graphite) was tested in different supporting electrolytes in the pH range of 1.0 to 13.0 to verify the useful potential window of the novel electrodic material. For this purpose, 0.1 mol L− 1 of H2SO4 (pH =1.0), NaOH (pH = 13), and KCl (pH = 7.0) solutions were evaluated using CV technique. Figure S3 A illustrates the cyclic voltammograms obtained in a potential window from −1.0 to +1.0 V, with a scan rate of 50 mV s− 1. Based on the voltammetric responses obtained at different solutions, it was possible to note that the composite material provides a stable background current almost in the entire potential range evaluated (−1.0 – 1.0 V), except for the NaOH solution that, as expected, presents an increased anodic current from 0.7 V related to oxidation of water/OH−. The cathodic peak around −0.6 V in the alkaline condition can be associated with the reduction of dissolved oxygen in the aqueous medium. Similar results were verified in other works using other thermoplastic composite devices [54,55].

The electrochemical performance of unmodified and modified Ecoflex:graphite sensor with GQDs and Au@GQDs was evaluated by the CV technique in the presence of 5.0 mmol L− 1 paraquat, which was used as surface-sensitive redox probe in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl. Figure S4 shows the CVs plots obtained with scan rate values ranging from 10 to 200 mV s− 1. Note that both anodic and cathodic peak currents showed linear behavior to the square root of the scan rate (ν1/2), demonstrating that the electrochemical sensor presents a diffusion mass-transport control. Using the Randles-Sevcik equation for a reversible reaction (Eq. (2)) [56,57], the active area of the electrodes presented values of 0.53 cm2, 0.79 cm2, and 1.13 cm2 for Ecoflex:graphite, Ecoflex:graphite/GQDs, and Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs, respectively. The geometric area of the composite electrodes was 0.25 cm2, thus the modification with the nanomaterials provided a 3.2-fold, and 4.5-fold increase in the electroactive area for the use of GQDs and Au@GQDs, respectively

Where Ip is the peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred (n = 1), A is the electroactive area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient of paraquat in 0.10 mol L− 1 KCl solution (1.12 × 10−5 cm2s − 1) [58], C is the concentration of paraquat (5 × 10−6 mol cm−3) and ν is the scan rate (V s− 1).

The values of peak potential separation (ΔEp) obtained by CVs using 5.0 mmol L− 1 paraquat were 68.91 ± 2.14 mV, 70.80 ± 3.16 mV, and 62.01 ± 1.26 mV, at 50 mV s− 1, for Ecoflex:graphite, Ecoflex:graphite/GQDs, Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensors, respectively. To further characterize the electrochemical behavior, we calculated the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k0) employing Nicholson's Equation [59] (Eq. (3)):

where ψ is the dimensionless kinetic parameter, π = 3.1415, F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J K− 1 mol−1), and T is the absolute temperature (298 K), n is the number of electrons transferred, D is the diffusion coefficient of paraquat in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl solution (1.12 × 10−5 cm2s− 1), v is the potential scan rate (V s− 1).

The values of ψ were obtained using the Eq. (4) proposed by Lavagnini et al. [60]:

We obtained k0 values of 2.34 × 10−2 cm s − 1, 1.95 × 10−2 cm s − 1 and 7.64 × 10−2 cm s− 1, for Ecoflex:graphite, Ecoflex:graphite/GQDs, Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs, respectively. To complement the electrochemical characterizations, we used the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique to determine the resistance to charge transfer (Rct) for all electrodes. Applying a Randles equivalent circuit (Figure S5) to fitting the Nyquist plots (Fig. 5 B), Rct values of 207 ± 13 Ω, 120 ± 9 Ω, and 27 ± 2 Ω were obtained for Ecoflex:graphite, Ecoflex:graphite/GQDs, Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs, respectively. The lowest Rct value and highest k0 value associated with the highest wettability properties of the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs indicates this electrode material as the best condition to be applied as an electrochemical sensor.

To evaluate the presence of the AuNP and the performance of the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs to detect glucose, we used the CV technique. Figure S6-A shows CVs plots obtained for Au@GQDs, recorded in 0.1 mol L− 1 KCl solution with a scan rate of 50 mV s− 1. An anodic peak at +900 mV and a cathodic peak at +400 mV were obtained and are related to the redox processes of the AuNP on the Ecoflex:graphite electrode (Figure S6-A) [61]. An irreversible oxidation peak at −130 mV was obtained by CV in the presence of 0.3 mol L− 1 glucose in 0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH (Figure S6-B), which was attributed to the electrocatalytic oxidation of glucose generated by Au@GQDs modified electrode in alkaline media. The alkaline condition was used based on the extensive literature reports that this condition provides a high interaction between glucose and gold surfaces providing catalytic electrooxidation of this compound [6,24,62].

Following, the effect of volume of Au@GQDs used as a modifier was evaluated for glucose sensing, Figure S3-B. Amperometric measurements using WE modified with different volumes of Au@GQDs (15, 40, 45, and 60 µL) were performed in 0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH solution containing glucose 1.5 mmol L− 1. The highest analytical signals were obtained for 15 and 30 µL of Au@GQDs and higher volumes provided a progressive decrease in the current intensity, probably associated with loss in the electroactive area due to the stacking of the nanomaterials. Based on the intensity and reproducibility of the electrochemical response, 30 µL of Au@GQDs to modify the thermoplastic composite sensor was selected. Aiming to obtain the best analytical conditions for glucose quantification, the potential applied in the amperometric measurements (0.0, −0.05, −0.10, −0.15, and −0.20 V) was also optimized, Figure S3-C. The best results were obtained applying −0.15 V and thus, this potential was further used for glucose sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of glucose using Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: As proof of potentiality, the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs electrochemical sensor was applied to detect glucose using the optimized conditions. Fig. 6 A illustrates the chronoamperogram obtained using a potential applied of −0.15 V for successive increments of glucose concentration in 0.1 mol L− 1 NaOH solution. As mentioned above, the alkaline electrolyte was chosen according to previous studies and corroborated with the literature [63–65]. The current values obtained from chronoamperograms measurements, recorded in triplicate, were extracted to obtain the analytical curve (Fig. 6B), which presented a linear behavior in a concentration ranging from 0.1 mmol L− 1 to 1.5 mmol L− 1 and with a sensitivity and linear regression (R2) of 4.85 µA L µmol−1 and 0.995, respectively.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were obtained through the equation
LOD
= (
(
y 0
−
y B
)
+ 3
s
y / x
) / S
in which:
(

−
y B
)
is the intercept value after subtraction of the analytical blank and Sy/x corresponds to the errors in the y-direction, S is the sensitivity parameter, and the
LOQ = 3.33
x
LOD
[61,66–68]. We obtained a LOD of 9.12 × 10−6 mol L− 1 and LOQ of 3.04 × 10−5 mol L− 1 using the proposed method.

The accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated through the addition and recovery protocol, by spiking different glucose levels (300 µmol L− 1 to 1.5 mmol L− 1) in synthetic saliva samples. The recovery values ranged from 97.84 to 105.20%, Table 1 , highlighting the excellent accuracy of the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs electrochemical sensor for the clinically relevant salivary glucose concentration range.

Table 2 summarizes some non-enzymatic electrochemical studies reported in the literature for glucose detection in several matrices. It is important to highlight that our method presents similar or even lower LOD compared to other studies, despite the simplicity and low-cost features of our manufacturing protocol, allowing it to be used as disposable devices (single use).

Reproducibility study using Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensor was carried out by amperometric measurements of 1.5 mmol L− 1 glucose using six different electrodes. A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.46% for glucose oxidation was obtained. It is worth highlighting that regardless of our fabrication method is not automatized, it presented adequate reproducibility in the manufacture of the electrodes.

The selectivity study was performed evaluating the response of potentially interfering compounds commonly found in saliva biofluid or after a meal. The concentrations of the potential interfering species (nitrite, tartrazine, sodium benzoate, sodium bisulfite, fructose, and ascorbic acid) were 0.3 mmol L− 1. Figure S7 illustrates the amperometric response obtained for each potential interferent using the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensor. It is important to note that fructose and ascorbic acid presented a significant interference in the glucose response of the sensor since they also oxidize at the experimental conditions used. However, these compounds are not expected to be found in appreciate concentrations in saliva samples compared to glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation studies of the composite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the degradation behavior of the material after being discarded, thermal degradation, photodegradation, and biodegradation tests were performed, Fig. 7 . TGA analysis was used to characterize the thermal degradation of the Ecoflex:graphite sensor components (Ecoflex®, graphite, and silver). It was observed that the thermal degradation of pure Ecoflex® starts at approximately 380 °C and ends at 440 °C and presented only a single decomposition process, Fig. 7A, corresponding to the decomposition of aliphatic polyester, adipic acid, and 1–4-butanediol. The mass loss of pure graphite was only 1.10% up to 900 °C. For silver from Ag/AgCl ink, the thermal degradation starts at approximately 390 °C and stabilizes at 450 °C, with a mass loss closed to 21%. Thus, the addition of these constituents in the synthesis of the composite promoted an improvement in the thermal stability of the thermoplastic material [69].

The monitoring of the photodegradation of Ecoflex® was performed by FTIR analysis for 192 h. Fig. 7B shows the spectra of the polymer before the exposure to UV-C light (0 h) and after 8 days of degradation (192 h). Measurements of 4 regions with different compositions were performed on the same sensor. However, only the Ecoflex material presented significant evidence of photodegradation. Based on the FTIR spectra, it is possible to observe a band growth in the region between 3300 - 3600 cm−1, region 1, characteristic of the OH stretch that was formed by the exposure of polymeric materials to photodegradation, probably by breaking of conjugated double bonds between carbon and oxygen, by UV-C light [70]. On the other hand, there is an attenuation of the intensity of the characteristic bands of Ecoflex® close to 2900 cm−1, and 1700 cm−1 from regions 2 and 3, respectively. This intensity decrease indicates that Ecoflex® was photodegraded. Similar behavior was observed for the region of 731 cm−1 whose band is attributed to the bending of the CH plane of the phenylene, indicating structural rearrangement in the polymer [38].

The simulation of the soil biodegradation process of the electrodes (Fig. 7C) was carried out for 6 months. Before being buried, 4 electrode samples were subjected to previous UV-C light treatment for 120 h to degrade the silver ink present in part of the electrode. Furthermore, exposure to light accelerates the degradation process of the Ecoflex® and brings the test conditions closer to what occurs in common sanitary landfills. In these places, the materials are dumped on the ground and are exposed to sunlight for a period until they are covered by the ground [71]. Fig. 7C-1 brings a digital image of the samples already photodegraded, inside a plastic petri dish with holes that were used to bury the sample to facilitate the identification of the material for a period of 6 months. After this period, the sample was dug up (Fig. 7C-2) and taken to the laboratory after removing the excess soil (Fig. 7C-3). In Fig. 7C-4 and 5, it is possible to visualize the electrode in the frontal and side profiles, respectively. By comparing the images of the biodegradation study, it is possible to verify the first signs of degradation of the electrodes, (Fig. 7C-4) through the loss of brightness, erosion on the surface of the sample, and loss of shape. The electrodes were not washed so as not to harm the biodegradation process on the surface. The surface erosion process is due to the microbial attack that leads to the beginning of the mass loss of the device [72]. Based on the literature, when thicker Ecoflex® pieces are used, as in this work, the hydrolysis may be restricted to the surface layers of the sample, possibly due to the aromatic fraction present in this biopolymer, which leads to a considerable reduction in the biodegradation rate of the electrodes [73].

Based on the thermal degradation, photodegradation, and biodegradation studies, it is possible to evidence the degradative behavior of the material as a whole. These studies help to predict the electrode's degradation profile after use, whether by disposal, incineration, or landfills, allowing us to envisage their environmental persistence and correct management for sustainable use. Collectively, the excellent electroanalytical characteristics of this cost-effective composite electrodic material associated with its biodegradability properties, allow us to indicate this thermoplastic material and manufacturing method for the development of disposable electrochemical sensors.

In this study, we fabricated a thermoplastic composite electrode using the biodegradable polymer (Ecoflex®) and graphite microparticles through the solvent-assisted technique. The manufacturing method proved to be simple and reproducible (RSD of 4.46%, n = 6 sensors). The thermoplastic composite electrode was modified with Au@GQDs for electrocatalytic detection of glucose. As a proof-of-potentiality, the Ecoflex:graphite/Au@GQDs sensor was applied to non-enzymatic glucose detection in alkaline media using an amperometric method. Under the optimized conditions, the sensor presented a linear concentration range from 0.1 mmol L− 1 to 1.5 mmol L− 1 glucose, with a LOD and LOQ of 9.1 µmol L− 1 and 30.4 µmol L− 1, respectively. The addition and recovery protocol in synthetic saliva samples provided recovery values ranging from 97.84 to 105.20%, highlighting the accuracy and robustness of the proposed method. These results allow us to indicate this thermoplastic composite sensor modified with Au@GQDs as an alternative method to salivary glucose sensing directly at the point of need.
